title: Jacob Johnson
text:
About Me
I’m a second year PhD student at the University of Wisconsin–Madison working with Dr. Matthias Katzfuß. I am also a year-round statistics intern at Sandia National Laboratories working with Dr. Katherine Goode.
My research is focused on finding new methods in Spatial Statistics, Gaussian Processes, and Uncertainty Quantification, specifically with applications in climate and environmental science.
Before starting my PhD, I did my undergrad at Brigham Young University where I did research with Dr. Matthew Heaton.
I’m originally from Lindon UT, and my hobbies include watching college sports, camping, hiking, and playing with my son.

title: Projects
text: Below are some non-peer-reviewed projects I’ve worked on. Each includes a short description and links to code, documents, etc.

title: Projects
text:
Functional Labeling of Wisconsin Environments
Understanding why ecosystems exhibit varying resilience to climate stress is a critical challenge in modern ecology. This study proposes a multi-step latent variable framework to examine modes of ecological vulnerability during the summer 2021 drought in Wisconsin. Utilizing high-resolution satellite imagery data, the proposed model decoupled 10-year historical environmental baselines from acute stress responses. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) condensed static landscape and climate variables, while Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) distilled collinear satellite metrics into two latent axes of stress response. Applying K-means clustering to these reduced dimensions naturally partitioned the state into three geographical vulnerability archetypes.
